learning_objectives: Explain how projects serve as vehicles for strategy execution
Map project objectives to strategic goals using a strategy map
Evaluate project alignment using balanced scorecard perspectives
readings: Srivannaboon, Linking Project Management with Business Strategy, Ch. 1-2
Kaplan & Norton, The Balanced Scorecard, Ch. 1

lecture_notes.introduction: Projects do not exist in isolation. Every project should be traceable to an organizational strategic objective. If it cannot be, it should be questioned.
key_concepts: Strategy execution gap: organizations formulate strategy but fail to execute through projects
Strategy map: visual linkage from mission to strategic themes to objectives to projects
Balanced scorecard perspectives: financial, customer, internal process, learning and growth
Strategic alignment: does this project move us toward our strategic goals?
Benefits realization: tracking whether projects deliver the intended strategic benefits
lecture_notes.demonstration: Mapping a portfolio of 10 projects to a company's strategic plan using a strategy alignment matrix

review.value: Visual tool showing cause-and-effect relationships from strategic objectives down to specific projects
Measures whether the strategic benefits promised in the business case actually materialize after project delivery

learning_objectives: Apply portfolio selection methods to prioritize projects
Use multi-criteria decision analysis for strategic project ranking
Balance strategic value, risk, and resource constraints
readings: PMI Standard for Portfolio Management, Ch. 4-5
Cooper, Winning at New Products, Ch. 7

lecture_notes.introduction: Organizations have more project ideas than resources. Strategic project selection ensures the right projects are funded.
key_concepts: Portfolio selection criteria: strategic alignment, financial return, risk level, resource availability
Multi-criteria decision analysis: weighted scoring across strategic dimensions
Bubble chart: plotting projects by strategic value vs. risk vs. investment size
Resource capacity planning: matching project demand to organizational capacity
Killing projects: the courage to stop projects that no longer align with strategy
lecture_notes.demonstration: Ranking 8 candidate projects using a weighted multi-criteria model with strategic alignment scores

review.value: Assigns weights to strategic criteria, scores each project, and ranks by weighted total; prevents single-factor dominance
Continuing a misaligned project wastes resources that could fund a strategically aligned one; sunk cost bias must be overcome

learning_objectives: Distinguish between project-level and strategic-level risks
Apply real options thinking to manage strategic uncertainty
Design risk governance structures for strategic projects
readings: Hillson, Managing Risk in Projects, Ch. 9-10

lecture_notes.introduction: Strategic projects operate in environments of deep uncertainty where traditional risk management may be insufficient.
key_concepts: Strategic risk: risks that affect the organization's ability to achieve its strategic objectives
Real options: treating investments as options that can be exercised, deferred, or abandoned
Stage-gate process: making go/no-go decisions at defined milestones with new information
Discovery-driven planning: testing assumptions before committing full resources
Strategic risk governance: board-level oversight, risk appetite setting, portfolio-level risk monitoring
lecture_notes.demonstration: Applying real options analysis to a technology investment decision with three possible outcomes

review.value: Invest small to learn, preserve the option to invest more or walk away; especially valuable under high uncertainty
Each gate is a go/no-go decision point with defined criteria; prevents escalation of commitment to failing projects

class_schedule.topic: Organizational Change and Strategic Transformation Projects
learning_objectives: Apply Kotter's 8-step change model to transformation projects
Design benefits realization plans for strategic initiatives
Manage the human side of strategic change
readings: PMI Managing Change in Organizations, Ch. 3-5

lecture_notes.introduction: Most strategic projects are change projects. Technical delivery is necessary but not sufficient; people must adopt the change for benefits to materialize.
key_concepts: Kotter's 8 steps: urgency, coalition, vision, communication, empowerment, quick wins, consolidation, anchoring
Benefits realization plan: mapping outputs to outcomes to benefits with measurement criteria
Change readiness assessment: organizational capacity and willingness to change
Transition management: moving from current state through transition to future state
Sustainability: ensuring changes stick after the project team disbands
lecture_notes.demonstration: Designing a benefits realization map for a digital transformation initiative

review.value: Project delivers outputs; outcomes are behavioral changes from using outputs; benefits are measurable improvements resulting from outcomes
Visible successes within 6-18 months build credibility and momentum for the broader transformation

class_schedule.topic: Governance and the Project Management Office (PMO)
learning_objectives: Design project governance structures appropriate to strategic projects
Compare PMO types: supportive, controlling, and directive
Implement governance mechanisms that enable rather than hinder project success
readings: PMI Governance of Portfolios, Programs, and Projects, Ch. 2-4

lecture_notes.introduction: Governance provides the framework for decision-making, oversight, and accountability. Good governance enables speed; bad governance creates bureaucracy.
key_concepts: Governance framework: decision rights, escalation paths, approval thresholds, reporting requirements
Steering committee: composition, charter, meeting cadence, decision-making authority
PMO types: supportive (templates and training), controlling (compliance and standards), directive (manages projects directly)
Governance tailoring: matching governance intensity to project risk and strategic importance
Governance anti-patterns: rubber-stamping, governance theater, over-governance of small projects
lecture_notes.demonstration: Designing a governance framework for a strategic transformation program with a steering committee charter

review.value: High-risk strategic projects need rigorous governance; small operational projects need lightweight governance; one-size-fits-all fails
A PMO adds value when it helps projects succeed, not when it creates paperwork; measure PMO by project outcomes, not compliance

class_schedule.topic: Strategic PM Maturity and Continuous Improvement
learning_objectives: Design a PM capability improvement roadmap
Measure the contribution of project management to strategic success
readings: OPM3: Organizational Project Management Maturity Model (PMI)
Kerzner, Strategic Planning for Project Management, Ch. 8-10

lecture_notes.introduction: Strategic PM is not a destination but a journey. Organizations must continuously improve their ability to deliver strategic value through projects.
key_concepts: Maturity models: OPM3, P3M3, Kerzner's 5-level model
Maturity assessment: current state evaluation across processes, people, tools, governance
Capability improvement roadmap: prioritized initiatives to advance maturity
Measuring PM value: project success rates, benefits realization, strategic goal achievement
Learning organization: systematic capture and application of project lessons learned
lecture_notes.demonstration: Conducting a maturity assessment using a simplified OPM3 questionnaire and creating an improvement plan

review.value: Ad hoc, Repeatable, Defined, Managed, Optimizing; most organizations are at level 2-3
Track project success rates, on-time/on-budget percentages, and most importantly, benefits realization rates

questions.question: A company has a strategic goal to 'increase market share in Asia by 20% within 3 years.' Three project proposals are on the table: (A) Build a new factory in Vietnam ($10M, 2-year payback), (B) Upgrade the US manufacturing plant ($5M, 1-year payback), (C) Launch a marketing campaign in Southeast Asia ($2M, uncertain payback). Rank these projects by strategic alignment and justify your ranking.
questions.answer: Ranking: 1st - Project A (factory in Vietnam) directly supports the strategic goal by establishing manufacturing presence in Asia, reducing shipping costs to Asian customers, and enabling local market responsiveness. 2nd - Project C (SE Asia marketing) directly targets the Asian market but depends on having product availability and competitive pricing. 3rd - Project B (US plant upgrade) has the best payback but does not directly support the Asia strategy; it optimizes existing operations. Strategic alignment should override financial metrics when projects compete for resources.

rubric.key: Justification linked to specific strategic goal
Explains why financial return alone is insufficient

questions.question: Explain how 'real options thinking' changes the way a project manager approaches a highly uncertain strategic initiative compared to traditional NPV analysis.
questions.answer: Traditional NPV forces an all-or-nothing decision: commit all resources now based on forecasted cash flows, or reject the project. Under high uncertainty, the forecasts are unreliable, and NPV often rejects valuable but uncertain projects. Real options thinking invests a small amount initially to learn (like buying an option), then makes further investment decisions as uncertainty resolves. For example, instead of committing $5M to a new market, invest $500K in a pilot. If the pilot succeeds, exercise the option to invest more. If not, the loss is limited to $500K rather than $5M. This preserves flexibility and values the ability to learn before committing.

rubric.key: Correctly describes NPV limitation under uncertainty

questions.question: A digital transformation project has delivered a new CRM system on time and on budget. However, 6 months later, only 30% of sales staff are using it regularly. Using the outputs-outcomes-benefits framework, explain what went wrong.
questions.answer: The project delivered the output (CRM system) successfully. However, the outcome (sales staff adopting and using the CRM in their daily work) was not achieved because adoption is a behavioral change that requires more than just delivering technology. Without training, change management, management reinforcement, and demonstrated value to users, the outcome gap means the strategic benefit (improved customer insights and sales effectiveness) cannot be realized. The project should have included a benefits realization plan with adoption metrics, training programs, and ongoing support. Delivering the technology on time and on budget is a necessary but insufficient condition for strategic success.

rubric.key: Correctly distinguishes output, outcome, and benefit
Recommends what should have been done

questions.question: Compare a supportive PMO and a directive PMO. Under what organizational conditions would each be most effective?
questions.answer: A supportive PMO provides templates, training, best practices, and mentoring but does not control projects. It is most effective when project managers are experienced and competent, the organizational culture values autonomy, and projects are diverse enough that standardized control would be counterproductive. A directive PMO directly manages projects, assigns project managers, and controls methodology. It is most effective when the organization has low PM maturity, project managers need significant oversight, projects are similar enough to benefit from standardized processes, or strategic projects require centralized control for resource allocation. Starting with a directive PMO and transitioning to supportive as maturity grows is a common pattern.

major_assignments.title: Strategic Project Portfolio Analysis and Governance Design
major_assignments.description: Analyze a portfolio of projects for strategic alignment, design a governance framework, and create a benefits realization plan for the highest-priority strategic initiative.
objectives: Evaluate 8-10 projects for strategic alignment using multi-criteria analysis
Create a portfolio prioritization with resource allocation recommendations
Design a governance framework with steering committee charter
Develop a benefits realization plan for the top-priority project
deliverables: Portfolio strategic alignment analysis with multi-criteria scoring (4-5 pages)
Portfolio prioritization bubble chart and resource allocation plan
Governance framework document with steering committee charter (3-4 pages)
Benefits realization plan with benefits map and measurement criteria (3-4 pages)

criteria: Multi-criteria model has relevant strategic dimensions with appropriate weights
All projects scored consistently and objectively
Governance structure is appropriate for the portfolio complexity
Decision rights and escalation paths clearly defined
Steering committee charter is complete and actionable
Benefits map clearly links outputs to outcomes to benefits
Measurement criteria are specific and measurable
Analysis is thorough and demonstrates strategic thinking

weekly_quizzes.description: Quizzes on strategic alignment, governance, and benefits realization

strategic_project.description: Portfolio analysis and governance design assignment

midterm_examination.description: Strategic alignment, project selection, and real options

final_examination.description: Cumulative exam covering all strategic PM topics

class_participation.description: Participation in case studies, portfolio exercises, and governance discussions

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is expected for all sessions. Case study discussions require preparation and participation.
course_policies.late_submissions: Assignments lose 10% per day late. No submissions accepted more than 5 days past the due date.
course_policies.collaboration: Case study discussions are collaborative. The strategic analysis assignment is individual work.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All work must be original. Strategic frameworks must be properly cited. Use of real company data requires anonymization.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Spreadsheet application for multi-criteria analysis and portfolio visualization. Presentation software for governance documents.
